name: Risperdal Boys
description: The "Risperdal Boys" refers to a series of lawsuits filed against Johnson & Johnson (J&J), the manufacturer of the antipsychotic drug Risperdal (risperidone). These lawsuits, primarily filed in the United States, allege that Risperdal caused gynecomastia (enlargement of male breast tissue) in boys and young men. The core claim is that J&J knew about this potential side effect but downplayed or concealed it while aggressively marketing the drug for off-label uses in children and adolescents, particularly for behavioral issues. Proponents argue that internal J&J documents and marketing strategies demonstrate a deliberate effort to profit at the expense of vulnerable individuals. They cite studies linking Risperdal to increased prolactin levels, which can trigger gynecomastia. Mainstream medical perspective acknowledges gynecomastia as a potential side effect of Risperdal, particularly at higher doses and in certain populations. However, the degree to which J&J actively concealed information or deliberately targeted children is a matter of legal contention and ongoing debate. Critics point out that the link between Risperdal and gynecomastia was documented in the drug's labeling, though perhaps not prominently enough. The lawsuits resulted in significant settlements and legal judgments against J&J, but the company maintains that it acted responsibly and denies any deliberate wrongdoing. The case highlights the complexities of pharmaceutical marketing, drug safety, and the potential conflicts between profit and patient well-being.
subtitle: Gynecomastia lawsuits against Johnson & Johnson

claims.evidence: Clinical studies showing increased prolactin levels and gynecomastia in Risperdal users, expert testimony in lawsuits.
claims.criticism: Gynecomastia is a known side effect, but its prevalence and severity vary. Other factors can also contribute to gynecomastia.

claims.claim: Johnson & Johnson knew about the gynecomastia risk but concealed it.
claims.evidence: Internal J&J documents allegedly showing awareness of the risk and strategies to downplay it.
claims.criticism: J&J argues that the risk was documented in the drug's labeling and denies deliberate concealment. Interpretation of internal documents is disputed.

claims.claim: J&J aggressively marketed Risperdal for off-label uses in children.
claims.evidence: Marketing materials targeting pediatric psychiatrists, sales tactics promoting off-label use for behavioral issues.
claims.criticism: Off-label use is legal and sometimes medically appropriate. J&J claims its marketing was within legal and ethical boundaries.

keyFigures.name: Aaron Kheriaty
keyFigures.role: Psychiatrist and critic of off-label Risperdal prescriptions.

keyFigures.name: David Kessler
keyFigures.role: Former FDA Commissioner who oversaw investigations into J&J's marketing practices.

mythologyConnections: Possible connection to themes of corporate greed and deception, reminiscent of mythical tricksters.

timeline.event: Risperdal approved by the FDA for schizophrenia in adults.
Increased off-label use of Risperdal in children and adolescents for behavioral issues.
Wave of lawsuits filed against J&J alleging Risperdal caused gynecomastia.
J&J fined $2.2 billion for illegally marketing Risperdal for off-label uses.

sources.author: U.S. Department of Justice
sources.work: Johnson & Johnson to Pay More Than $2.2 Billion to Resolve Criminal and Civil Investigations
sources.title: U.S. Department of Justice

sources.author: Bloomberg News
sources.work: Johnson & Johnson Ordered to Pay $8 Billion in Risperdal Case
sources.title: Bloomberg News
